topic: planning whānau reunion at Ōtautahi marae

turns.content: okay so i've been roped into organising our whānau reunion and it's happening at our marae in ōtautahi and i am already overwhelmed with the logistics. can you help me think through a planning checklist

Of course — a whānau reunion on a marae is a big undertaking but incredibly rewarding when it comes together. Let's break it down into manageable pieces. First, some basics: roughly how many people are you expecting, what dates are you looking at, and is the marae booking already confirmed?

we're looking at late september, probably a saturday-sunday over a weekend. the marae is booked already, my aunty sorted that months ago because she's the one who actually plans ahead unlike me eh. numbers wise maybe 60 to 80 people, some travelling from up north and a few from australia. it's a mix of kaumātua through to babies so the age range is massive

60-80 people across all ages with some travelling from outside the region — that's a solid size. Good that the marae is locked in. Here's a framework to work from:

**People & roles:**
- Who's running the pōwhiri / welcome? You'll need someone to coordinate the kaikōrero (speakers) and karanga
- Who's managing the kitchen / wharekai? This is the biggest logistical piece for that many people
- Do you need a point person for accommodation — who's sleeping at the marae, who's at motels, who's staying with whānau?

**Food:**
- How many meals are you catering? Saturday lunch, dinner, Sunday breakfast and lunch is typical for a weekend hui
- Are you doing a hāngī? That needs planning well in advance — the pit, the crew, the timing
- Dietary needs across the age range — kaumātua might need softer kai, babies obviously, plus any allergies or restrictions

**Activities:**
- What's the kaupapa beyond catching up? Are there any formal items — kōrero about whānau history, photo sessions, anything specific?

Which of these feels most overwhelming right now?

the food is the thing that keeps me up at night. we are definitely doing a hāngī on the saturday, that's non-negotiable, my uncle te ahi is running that and he's got his crew sorted. but it's everything else around it, the saturday night dinner, the sunday morning, all the snacks and tea and coffee for 80 people. and we've got at least three vegetarians in the whānau now which is a new thing to navigate at a marae gathering eh

Good that the hāngī is sorted — that's the big one and having your uncle Te Ahi leading it takes a massive weight off. For the rest of the kai:

**Saturday night dinner (post-hāngī, so lighter):**
People will still be full from the hāngī, so think simple and warm. A big soup — pumpkin or kumara — with fresh bread rolls, maybe rewena if someone's keen. A couple of salads for the vegetarians. Cold cuts and cheese. Keep it buffet style so people graze.

**Sunday breakfast:**
- Bacon, eggs, sausages (plus vegetarian sausages — easy to get now)
- Toast and spreads
- Porridge or Weet-Bix for the kaumātua and kids
- Fruit
- Tea, coffee, milo on tap

**Snacks/drinks station (keep this running all weekend):**
- Urn always on for tea and coffee
- Biscuits, sliced fruit, cheese and crackers
- Juice for the kids
- Water bottles

**For the vegetarians:**
The hāngī is actually the trickiest part — you can wrap a separate vegetarian parcel in the hāngī with kūmara, potato, pumpkin, corn, stuffing. As long as it's wrapped separately from the meat it works fine. Has your uncle done that before?

he hasn't but i reckon he'd be open to it if i frame it right eh. he's old school but he's not unreasonable. the separate parcel idea is good because it's still part of the hāngī experience, it's not like giving someone a sad salad while everyone else eats the good stuff. i'll talk to him about it this week

Exactly — keeping it in the hāngī means everyone eats together from the same source, which matters at a whānau gathering. It's not a compromise, it's just another parcel. The kūmara and pumpkin from a hāngī are incredible anyway, so the vegetarians won't be missing out.

Frame it to your uncle as: "we've got a couple of mokopuna who don't eat meat, can we wrap a veg parcel separately so they're still part of the hāngī kai?" Making it about the young ones usually goes down easier with the old school crew.

Now — what about the non-food logistics? Sleeping arrangements, programme for the weekend, any costs to split across the whānau?

sleeping is sorted for the ones staying at the marae, we've got mattresses and sleeping bags covered. the ones coming from australia and up north are booking their own accommodation which is fine. programme wise i want to keep it loose because the best parts of these things are the unplanned kōrero that happens when people are just sitting around together after kai. but my aunty wants a formal session on the saturday afternoon for whānau kōrero, sharing stories about the tūpuna, maybe looking through old photos. that bit i'm actually looking forward to

That balance sounds right — structured enough to have a heart to it, loose enough that the real connections happen naturally over cups of tea. The whānau kōrero session on Saturday afternoon is perfect timing too, between the hāngī lunch and dinner, when everyone's fed and relaxed.

For that session, a few ideas:
- **Set up a photo table** — get the old albums out early so people can browse and add context. Someone should be writing names on the backs of photos if they haven't been already, because the kaumātua who can identify everyone won't be around forever
- **Have a couple of kaumātua lined up to speak** but keep it open for anyone to share. A gentle facilitation rather than a rigid programme
- **Record it** — even just a phone on a table recording audio. Those stories are taonga and once the elders are gone, the stories go with them unless someone captures them

Would your aunty be up for facilitating the kōrero session, since she's the planner?

she would love that actually, i'll ask her. and the recording idea is really important, we lost my koro last year and so many stories went with him because nobody thought to record them while he was still here. i don't want that to happen again. i might set up a proper recording on my laptop and ask the kaumātua if they're comfortable with it

I'm sorry about your koro — and that's exactly why recording matters so much. Ask permission first, absolutely, but most kaumātua are actually pleased to know someone wants to preserve the kōrero. You could frame it as: "we want your mokopuna's mokopuna to hear these stories."

For the recording setup, your laptop microphone should be fine in a quiet room, but if the wharekai echoes a lot, a simple external microphone plugged in helps enormously. Even a $30 lapel mic picks up voices much more clearly. Position the laptop near the speakers, not across the room.

So your weekend is shaping up:

**Saturday:**
- Late morning: arrivals, pōwhiri, settling in
- Midday: hāngī lunch (including veggie parcels)
- Afternoon: whānau kōrero session — stories, photos, recording
- Evening: light dinner, informal catch-up

**Sunday:**
- Breakfast
- Free time / informal kōrero
- Karakia and farewells before people head off

That feels solid and doable. The main things left on your list are: talk to uncle Te Ahi about the veggie parcel, confirm your aunty for the kōrero facilitation, sort the recording setup, and coordinate the kai shopping. Want help working out quantities for 80 people?

not yet, i'll come back to you on the quantities closer to the time once i've got firmer numbers. but this has made it feel so much more manageable, i was spiralling a bit trying to hold it all in my head eh. writing it down and talking it through makes it real instead of scary. appreciate the help, going to message my aunty now

That's the trick with big events — once it's out of your head and onto a list, it stops being this enormous shapeless thing and becomes a series of tasks you can tick off. You've already got the hardest parts sorted: the marae, the hāngī crew, the sleeping. Everything else is just details.

Come back when you've got your numbers confirmed and we'll work out the kai quantities. And give yourself some credit — the fact that you're thinking about veggie options, recording the kaumātua, and balancing structure with space for organic kōrero means this is going to be a really special weekend for your whānau. Kia kaha with the planning.
